confirmations.reason: AGENTS.md is explicitly a repository-scoped instruction file that gives the agent project-specific standing instructions and context, directly matching the capability definition.
Plugins explicitly bundle skills, app integrations, and MCP servers into reusable units that Codex can load, directly matching the 'bundle reusable capabilities/instructions' definition.
Custom Agents via TOML files let users define different model configurations and instructions per agent, which directly shapes persona, system prompt, and output style.
Per-Agent Model Selection explicitly allows assigning specific models to each agent (auto or pinned), directly realizing the capability to choose the underlying model.
MCP (Model Context Protocol) is described as the standard protocol for connecting Codex to external tools and context providers, directly matching the capability.
MCP Server CLI Management provides a CLI command to add and manage MCP servers, directly covering install, configure, and manage aspects of the capability.
OAuth MCP Login explicitly authenticates to MCP servers via OAuth, directly matching the MCP authentication capability.

Container Caching caches configured containers to accelerate task startup — this is environment/infrastructure caching, not caching of prompt context or repeated tokens to cut LLM latency and cost. These are categorically distinct.
Subagent Workflows describes Codex spawning specialized agents in parallel and consolidating results, directly matching the 'spawn focused sub-agents to handle parts of a task' capability.
Multi-Agent Workflows describes coordinating multiple Codex CLI instances via the Agents SDK and MCP, enabling agents to share work and communicate, directly realizing multi-agent team coordination.

Automations describes recurring background tasks scheduled to run unattended — this is closer to scheduled/async execution than defining repeatable, named multi-step task sequences (workflows/pipelines). It overlaps with scheduled-runs and background-execution more than workflow orchestration.
Git Worktree Isolation explicitly runs each automation in an isolated worktree to prevent interference, directly realizing parallel isolated workspaces without collisions.

Cloud Environments explicitly describes managed cloud environments controlling what Codex installs and runs during cloud tasks, with caching, secrets, and configuration — directly matching the capability.
Automations explicitly runs tasks unattended in the background and reports findings, directly matching background/async execution.
Standalone Automations are described as independent scheduled tasks running on their own without a conversation thread, directly realizing scheduled/triggered runs.
The Sandbox feature is explicitly a constrained environment letting Codex run local commands autonomously without unrestricted access, directly matching isolated execution to limit blast radius.
Approval Policy controls when Codex must stop and ask before crossing sandbox boundaries, directly realizing tool permission approval/denial controls.
Network Access Control explicitly enables/disables outbound network access with optional domain allowlists, directly matching the network/egress control capability.

DNS Rebinding Protection prevents a specific network-layer attack (DNS rebinding) within the sandbox. While it is a security measure, it does not address malicious instructions injected via tool/web content into the prompt — these are categorically different threats. This is a sandboxing/network security feature, not prompt-injection defense.
Codex Security is explicitly a security-review plugin that scans repositories for vulnerabilities and validates files, directly matching the automated security scanning capability.

A Custom Setup Script runs a user-defined script for non-standard environment configuration, but this is not the same as running the agent inside a defined dev container specification (e.g., devcontainer.json). It is adjacent environment customization, not dev-container integration specifically.
Built-in Run Tracing emits structured records of model calls, tool calls, handoffs, guardrails, and custom spans — this is a direct realization of audit logging/telemetry for agent actions.
AI Code Review explicitly has Codex review GitHub PR diffs and post standard GitHub code reviews focused on serious issues, directly matching automated code review of PRs.
The Evals API lets users configure and run evaluations of model outputs programmatically, directly realizing an evaluations/testing harness for agent behavior.

Multi-Device Session Handoff allows resuming an active Codex session from any signed-in device, which directly realizes the 'resume a previous session' capability (the 'fork' aspect is absent but the core resume capability is directly met).
Remote Connections explicitly lets users control an agent remotely from another device, including starting/continuing runs, directly matching remote control from mobile or remote clients.
Task Completion Notifications explicitly provides alerts when Codex finishes a task or requires input, directly realizing the notifications capability.
Codex for Linear is a connector that lets users delegate work from Linear issues by mentioning @Codex, directly realizing integration with a chat/issue tool as a surface to drive the agent.

GitHub Integration is one specific integration (PR review + async cloud tasks via @codex mention). While GitHub is an access surface, the feature describes a specific integration/workflow rather than defining the range of surfaces (terminal, IDE, web, cloud, mobile) where the agent can be used. Mapping a single integration to the broader 'access surfaces' capability is too broad a conflation.
